(credit: modification of work by John Taylor) 1.3 Physical and Chemical Properties By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Identify properties of and changes in matter as physical or chemical • Identify properties of matter as extensive or intensive The characteristics that enable us to distinguish one substance from another are called properties. A physical property is a characteristic of matter that is not associated with a change in its chemical composition. Familiar examples of physical properties include density, color, hardness, melting and boiling points, and electrical conductivity. We can observe some physical properties, such as density and color, without changing the physical state of the matter observed. Other physical properties, such as the melting temperature of iron or the freezing temperature of water, can only be observed as matter undergoes a physical change. A physical change is a change in the state or properties of matter without any accompanying change in its chemical composition (the identities of the substances contained in the matter). We observe a physical change when wax melts, when sugar dissolves in coffee, and when steam condenses into liquid water (Figure 1.18). Other examples of physical changes include magnetizing and demagnetizing metals (as is done with common antitheft security tags) and grinding solids into powders (which can sometimes yield noticeable changes in color). In each of these examples, there is a change in the physical stat

This video (http://openstaxcollege.org/l/16handwarmer) shows the crystallization process occurring in a hand warmer. 11.4 Colligative Properties By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Express concentrations of solution components using mole fraction and molality • Describe the effect of solute concentration on various solution properties (vapor pressure, boiling point, freezing point, and osmotic pressure) • Perform calculations using the mathematical equations that describe these various colligative effects • Describe the process of distillation and its practical applications • Explain the process of osmosis and describe how it is applied industrially and in nature The properties of a solution are different from those of either the pure solute(s) or solvent. Many solution properties are dependent upon the chemical identity of the solute. Compared to pure water, a solution of hydrogen chloride is more acidic, a solution of ammonia is more basic, a solution of sodium chloride is more dense, and a solution of sucrose is more viscous. There are a few solution properties, however, that depend only upon the total concentration of solute species, regardless of their identities. These colligative properties include vapor pressure lowering, boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, and osmotic pressure. This small set of properties is of central importance to many natural phenomena and technological applications, as will be described in this module. Mole Fraction and Molality Several units commonly used to express the concentrations of solution components were

Figure 2.26 Elements in the periodic table are organized according to their properties. Many elements differ dramatically in their chemical and physical properties, but some elements are similar in their behaviors. For example, many elements appear shiny, are malleable (able to be deformed without breaking) and ductile (can be drawn into wires), and conduct heat and electricity well. Other elements are not shiny, malleable, or ductile, and are poor conductors of heat and electricity. We can sort the elements into large classes with common properties: metals (elements that are shiny, malleable, good conductors of heat and electricity—shaded yellow); nonmetals (elements that appear dull, poor conductors of heat and electricity—shaded green); and metalloids (elements that conduct heat and electricity moderately well, and possess some properties of metals and some properties of nonmetals—shaded purple). The elements can also be classified into the main-group elements (or representative elements) in the columns labeled 1, 2, and 13–18; the transition metals in the columns labeled 3–12; and inner transition metals in the two rows at the bottom of the table (the top-row elements are called lanthanides and the bottom-row elements are actinides; Figure 2.27). The elements can be subdivided further by more specific properties, such as the composition of the compounds they form. For example, the elements in group 1 (the first column) form compounds that consist of one atom of the element and one atom of hydrogen. These elements (except hydrogen) are known as alkali metals, and they al

supplements.curriculum_md_raw:
# 一氧化碳的性质

## 基本信息
- **学科**: 化学
- **学段**: 初中
- **年级**: 九年级
- **章节**: 碳和碳的氧化物
- **知识点**: 一氧化碳的性质
- **课标版本**: 义务教育课程标准（2022年版）

---

## 一、课标原文

### 1.1 内容要求
> "了解一氧化碳的性质（毒性、还原性、可燃性），认识一氧化碳与二氧化碳之间的转化关系；了解一氧化碳对环境和人体健康的危害.

### 1.2 学业要求
> 能列举一氧化碳的主要性质及其危害；能写出碳、一氧化碳、二氧化碳之间相互转化的化学方程式；能从氧化还原反应的角度分析一氧化碳的还原性；能提出防范一氧化碳中毒的措施.

### 1.3 教学提示
> （1）通过实验演示或模拟实验展示一氧化碳的性质，强调安全操作规范。

（2）联系冬季取暖煤气中毒案例，加强安全教育。

（3）引导学生从化合价变化的角度理解一氧化碳的还原性。

（4）讨论如何减少一氧化碳排放，培养环保意识。

---

## 二、教学目标

### 2.1 知识与技能
- 学生能够理解一氧化碳的性质的基本概念和原理
- 学生能够正确进行相关计算和推理
- 学生能够识别和描述相关的概念、现象或材料
- 学生能够理解一氧化碳的性质的基本概念和原理

### 2.2 过程与方法
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习化学的方法
- 学生能够运用化学学科方法解决问题
- 学生能够在交流中表达自己的思路
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习化学的方法

### 2.3 情感态度与价值观
- 学生感受化学与现实生活密切相关
- 学生体验化学探究的乐趣，树立学好化学的信心
- 学生养成认真细致、独立思考的学习习惯
- 学生感受化学与现实生活密切相关

---

## 三、教学重难点

### 3.1 教学重点
- 一氧化碳的性质的概念、性质和应用
- 形成完整的知识体系结构
- 一氧化碳的性质的概念、性质和应用

### 3.2 教学难点
- 掌握一氧化碳的性质的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用

supplements.textbook_summary:
## 教材化讲解参考（由课标要点生成）

### 核心概念
一氧化碳的性质是化学学习中的重要知识点。教学时应围绕课标要点“2.2.1 空气、氧气、二氧化碳：了解空气的主要成分，认识氧气、二氧化碳的主要性质和用途。”展开，先明确概念边界，再通过例子、图示、实验、语篇或案例帮助学生建立可迁移的理解。

### 学习路径
1. 情境导入：从实验现象、微观粒子和符号表达三个层面讲解「一氧化碳的性质」，让学生把现象与本质对应起来。
2. 探究建构：重视实验安全、证据记录、化学用语规范和条件限制，避免只背结论。
3. 应用迁移：围绕课标要点“2.2.1 空气、氧气、二氧化碳：了解空气的主要成分，认识氧气、二氧化碳的主要性质和用途。”，安排观察、解释、书写表达和应用判断任务。

### 例题设计方向
可设计一道“识别核心概念”的基础题，一道“解释现象或文本材料”的理解题，一道“迁移到真实情境”的应用题，并要求学生说明依据。

A physical property is a characteristic of matter that is not associated with a change in its chemical composition. Familiar examples of physical properties include density, color, hardness, melting and boiling points, and electrical conductivity. We can observe some physical properties, such as density and color, without changing the physical state of the matter observed. Other physical properties, such as the melting temperature of iron or the freezing temperature of water, can only be observed as matter undergoes a physical change. A physical change is a change in the state or properties of matter without any accompanying change in its chemical composition (the identities of the substances contained in the matter). We observe a physical change when wax melts, when sugar dissolves in coffee, and when steam condenses into liquid water (Figure 1.18). Other examples of physical changes include magnetizing and demagnetizing metals (as is done with common antitheft security tags) and grinding solids into powders (which can sometimes yield noticeable changes in color). Many solution properties are dependent upon the chemical identity of the solute. Figure 2.26 Elements in the periodic table are organized according to their properties. Many elements differ dramatically in their chemical and physical properties, but some elements are similar in their behaviors. For example, many elements appear shiny, are malleable (able to be deformed without breaking) and ductile (can be drawn into wires), and conduct heat and electricity well. Other elements are not shiny, malleable, or ductile, and are poor conductors of heat and electricity. We can sort the elements into large classes with common properties: metals (elements that are shiny, malleable, good conductors of heat and electricity—shaded yellow); nonmetals (elements that appear dull, poor conductors of heat and electricity—shaded green); and metalloids (elements that conduct heat and electricity moderately well, and possess some properties of metals and some properties of nonmetals—shaded purple). The elements can also be classified into the main-group elements (or representative elements) in the columns labeled 1, 2, and 13–18; the transition metals in the columns labeled 3–12; and inner transition metals in the two rows at the bottom of the table (the top-row elements are called lanthanides and the bottom-row elements are actinides; Figure 2.27). The elements can be subdivided further by more specific properties, such as the composition of the compounds they form. For example, the elements in group 1 (the first column) form compounds that consist of one atom of the element and one atom of hydrogen. These elements (except hydrogen) are known as alkali metals, and they al

can also be referred to by the first element of the group: For example, the chalcogens can be called the oxygen group or oxygen family. Hydrogen is a unique, nonmetallic element with properties similar to both group 1 and group 17 elements. For that reason, hydrogen may be shown at the top of both groups, or by itself. Figure 2.27 The periodic table organizes elements with similar properties into groups. Click on this link (http://openstaxcollege.org/l/16Periodic) for an interactive periodic table, which you can use to explore the properties of the elements (includes podcasts and videos of each element). You may also want to try this one that shows photos of all the elements. Example 2.7 Naming Groups of Elements Atoms of each of the following elements are essential for life. Give the group name for the following elements: (a) chlorine (b) calcium (c) sodium (d) sulfur Link to Learning

exercises.stem: 描述一个与「一氧化碳的性质」相关的实验现象，并用化学概念解释原因。
exercises.answer: 应扣住课标要点：2.2.1 空气、氧气、二氧化碳：了解空气的主要成分，认识氧气、二氧化碳的主要性质和用途。，从现象、微观本质和符号表达三个层面回答。

exercises.stem: 判断一个生活或实验场景是否体现「一氧化碳的性质」，并说明证据。
exercises.answer: 需写出观察证据、相关概念、判断依据和安全注意事项。

real_world: 从厨房、清洁用品、材料变化或环境问题中寻找与「一氧化碳的性质」相关的化学现象。
